%%
%% The "title" command has an optional parameter,
%% allowing the author to define a "short title" to be used in page headers.
\title[Restoring Physiological Awareness Among Remote Teammates]%
{Restoring Physiological Awareness Among Remote Teammates by Encoding Wearable Heart Rate and Variability as Peripheral Avatar Cues}

\author{Anonymous Author(s)}



%%
%% By default, the full list of authors will be used in the page
%% headers. Often, this list is too long, and will overlap
%% other information printed in the page headers. This command allows
%% the author to define a more concise list
%% of authors' names for this purpose.
\renewcommand{\shortauthors}{Anonymous Author(s)}

%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Networked multiplayer games are among the most inhabited yet distributed interactive spaces: teammates act together while sitting far apart, so bodily signals that colocated players read at a glance never cross the network. Prior work treats physiology either as emotional ground truth to classify or as felt closeness, leaving open whether a distant partner's state can be made readable in the shared interface as an ambient coordination cue. We present a body worn interface layer that renders Apple Watch heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV) as an animated halo around each player avatar: pulse and brightness encode cardiovascular activation, ring coherence encodes autonomic variability. Informed by Science and Technology Studies, where measurement and classification constitute rather than report what they name \cite{nelson_meeting_1996, bowker_sorting_2008, haraway_situated_1988}, the halo withholds emotion words, offering graded, situated cues. An online study with 36 distributed participants, joining ISO 9186 comprehension, the Self Assessment Manikin, and a workload check, read HR at 84 percent and HRV at 81 percent from animation alone, ordered as designed and at low effort. We contribute a wearable technique, an STS visualization method, and evidence that ambient bodily cues steady social coordination across an interactive space.
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}
Networked multiplayer games have become one of the largest settings where people cooperate with others they cannot see. Inside a single match, strangers and friends assemble into teams, divide roles, and steer shared outcomes through fast rounds of coordination. What holds such teams together is communication, and its quality tracks how well they perform: analyses of large player populations show that the volume and structure of team communication separate strong clans from weak ones \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and even a channel as thin as a map ping measurably shifts the result of a competitive match \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}. Coordination in these spaces rests on a stream of small signals that tell each member what the others attend to and how hard they are working.

One signal that teammates in a shared room read without effort never survives the trip across the network. When people occupy the same space, they register a partner's quickened breath, restless posture, and rising tension continuously and prereflectively, and they fold that reading into how they speak and act \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}. Distributed play strips this bodily layer away. A remote partner must infer effort, composure, and withdrawal from text, voice, and avatar motion, which carry far less than shared physical presence does. Controlled comparison confirms the cost: when the cues available in face to face contact are removed, collaborators understand one another less well, and judge one another more harshly, than when a representation of the partner's bodily state is restored \cite{tan_role_2014}.

The consequence is a specific failure of interpretation rather than a general lack of information. A teammate who has gone quiet may be concentrating, overloaded, or withdrawing, and the three call for opposite responses. Absent any read on the body, players guess, and wrong guesses accumulate into misattributed blame, mistimed help, and coordination that unravels under pressure. The problem this paper addresses is how to return a usable trace of that missing bodily layer to distributed teammates.

Sending physiological data across a network is not new. Heart rate has been carried into chat to raise empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, into shared displays to deepen social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, and into cooperative and virtual games to strengthen the felt bond between players \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}. Two limits recur. First, a long affective computing tradition treats a biosignal as evidence for an underlying emotion to be detected and named \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, fixing a single interpretation onto a body whose signals are notoriously ambiguous. Second, studies that report warmer feelings between partners seldom test whether a viewer can actually read the state on display and decide what to do about it, or whether the representation communicates anything once its emotion labels are removed. Legibility, and the situated judgment that follows from it, stays largely unmeasured.

We take a different route, and its shape comes from Science and Technology Studies. If measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears merely to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, if the categories an interface imposes carry consequences of their own \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and if every reading of a body belongs to a particular viewer and moment \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, then an interface that stamps a teammate's heartbeat with the word calm or panic overreaches. Our display withholds such verdicts. It renders Apple Watch heart rate as pulse rate and brightness, standing for cardiovascular activation, and heart rate variability as the coherence and steadiness of a ring, standing for autonomic regulation \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}. These become graded, deliberately open cues that a teammate weighs against the unfolding match rather than reads off as fact \cite{suchman_plans_1987, wakkary_things_2021}. The artifact is built to make an argument, not a diagnosis.

We evaluated whether that argument is even readable. In an online study, 36 distributed participants viewed all sixteen combinations of four heart rate states and four variability states and reported what each conveyed, through established instruments for symbol comprehension, affective rating, and workload. Participants recovered heart rate at 84 percent and variability at 81 percent from animation alone, well past the 25 percent expected from guessing. Their interpretations were ordered as the design intended: perceived urgency and arousal rose with activation, while falling variability coherence lowered perceived stability and raised the intention to offer support, none of it routed through emotion words. Reading the display cost little effort, most participants would accept it in cooperative play, and several drew boundaries around exposing bodily data to teammates, a concern we take up directly.

We contribute:
\begin{itemize}
\item a peripheral avatar display that turns wrist worn heart rate and variability into an ambient layer for reading a distant teammate, designed for the shared interactive space of a multiplayer match;
\item a design method that translates Science and Technology Studies commitments about measurement, classification, and situated knowledge into concrete visual choices, producing a representation that offers social cues without assigning emotions;
\item empirical evidence that this label free representation is legible, interpreted in a graded and consistent way, and light enough in workload to be usable, together with the privacy limits players place around it.
\end{itemize}

\section{Literature Review}

\subsection{Communication and Coordination in Distributed Play}
Multiplayer games reward study less as game systems than as places where scattered groups organize themselves. Communication volume and structure separate skilled teams from weak ones across large player populations \cite{bisberg_communication_2025}, and a signal as spare as a positional ping alters who wins a competitive round \cite{leavitt_ping_2016}. The channels behind these findings are deliberate and symbolic: messages, calls, pings. The involuntary bodily signals that regulate teamwork in a shared room, the ones a partner registers before either speaks \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, reach the game through no channel at all. This section traces how prior work has tried to carry the body across a network, and where each attempt stops short of the reading a teammate needs.

\subsection{Reading Physiology as Emotion}
One tradition treats the body as a store of emotion to be recovered by computation. Affective computing set the agenda of sensing a signal and inferring the feeling behind it \cite{picard_affective_1997}, and play research applied it directly, mapping cardiac, muscular, and electrodermal activity onto models of a player's emotional state \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007, ravaja_phasic_2006} and adjusting game difficulty from those readings \cite{nacke_biofeedback_2011}. Surveys record both the reach of this approach and its persistent snag \cite{gan_human-computer_2022}: one elevated heart rate accompanies fear, exertion, and delight alike, so a system that prints a single emotion word onto it throws away the ambiguity the body genuinely carries. That interpretive move is the one our design declines.

\subsection{Sharing Physiology Between People}
A second line sends the signal to another person rather than to a classifier. Heart rate has been shared to build social connection \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, moge_shared_2022}, folded into mobile chat to lift empathy and awareness \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017}, and visualized during remote collaboration, where it raised group cohesion and perceived warmth over voice alone \cite{tan_role_2014}. In play, coupled and visualized biosignals have deepened the bond between partners in cooperative and virtual settings \cite{jarvela_augmented_2021, hirsch_my_2023, jiang_intimasea_2023, muller_intra-_2013}, consistent with evidence that physiological alignment tracks social closeness \cite{dikker_brain--brain_2017}. This work shows convincingly that a shared bodily signal changes how people relate. It leaves two things untested. It seldom asks whether a viewer can name the state on show, as distinct from feeling closer to its owner, and it seldom removes the emotional framing to check that the representation still communicates. Legibility, the precondition for acting on a cue mid match, is rarely what these studies measure.

\subsection{Situated Interpretation and a Science and Technology Studies Stance}
Where the first tradition locates meaning in the signal and the second in the bond, a third locates it in the moment of use. Situated and embodied accounts hold that action is read against an unfolding context rather than decoded from a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, and design research treats an artifact as a way to pose that question rather than close it \cite{wakkary_things_2021}. Science and Technology Studies sharpens the stakes for anything that measures a body: instruments help produce the phenomena they seem only to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, classifications carry consequences for those they sort \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and no reading of a body is a view from nowhere \cite{haraway_situated_1988}. Together these commitments argue against an interface that resolves a teammate's heartbeat into a verdict, and for one that offers a graded, open cue to be interpreted in place.

\subsection{Positioning the Present Work}
Two differences define this work against that background. Where shared physiology systems almost always transmit heart rate by itself, we add heart rate variability as a separate visual dimension, because the two indices carry different information: rate reflects momentary activation, while variability reflects the autonomic regulation underneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007}. And where earlier displays are judged by the connection they produce, we judge ours by the reading it supports, testing whether a label free representation is legible before asking what it does socially. Table~\ref{tab:related} sets the contrast out in full.

\begin{table*}[t]
\footnotesize
\caption{Systems that carry physiological data between people, and how the present work differs. \emph{Emotion labels} marks whether the display names a feeling (Named), shows a raw or connection oriented signal that implies one (Implicit), or names none (None). \emph{Reader legibility} marks whether the study tested that a viewer can identify the intended state from the display itself.}
\label{tab:related}
\begin{tabular}{@{}l p{2.4cm} p{3.2cm} p{1.3cm} p{1.6cm}@{}}
\toprule
Work & Signal and setting & Primary aim & Emotion labels & Reader legibility tested \\
\midrule
Picard \cite{picard_affective_1997} & Multiple signals, no shared display & Recognize and model affect by computation & Named & No \\
Mandryk and Atkins \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007} & Cardiac, muscular, electrodermal in play & Infer a player's emotion from physiology & Named & No \\
Slov\'ak et al. \cite{slovak_understanding_2012} & Heart rate, shared display & Support social connection & Implicit & No \\
Hassib et al. \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017} & Heart rate, mobile chat & Raise empathy and awareness & Implicit & No \\
Tan et al. \cite{tan_role_2014} & Physiological signals, remote collaboration & Restore awareness and cohesion at a distance & Implicit & Indirect \\
Moge et al. \cite{moge_shared_2022} & Heart rate, cooperative game & Enrich shared play & Implicit & No \\
Hirsch et al. \cite{hirsch_my_2023} & Heartbeat, avatar in a VR game & Increase social connectedness & Implicit & No \\
\textbf{This work} & Heart rate and variability, peripheral avatar cue in distributed play & Make a teammate's state readable as a coordination cue & None & Yes \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}
\section{Methodology}
\noindent
We used a research through design methodology with two phases. In the design phase, we derived the icon system from Science and Technology Studies. In the evaluation phase, we tested the resulting visual language for legibility and interpretation in a controlled online study. The subsections below state the research questions and hypotheses, then describe the design rationale, the icon system that stood as the study material, the study design, the participants, the apparatus, the procedure, the measures, and the analysis.

\subsection{Research Questions and Hypotheses}
We addressed three research questions.
\begin{description}
    \item[RQ1] Can players identify a teammate's heart rate state and heart rate variability state from the animated icon alone, without numbers or emotion words?
    \item[RQ2] Do the two visual channels carry the graded social meaning they were designed to carry, that is, does activation read as urgency and arousal, and does declining ring coherence read as lower stability and a stronger call for support?
    \item[RQ3] Is the display clear and light enough in workload to be usable during cooperative play?
\end{description}

We set five hypotheses.
\begin{description}
    \item[H1] Identification accuracy for both the heart rate state and the variability state exceeds the 25 percent chance level of a choice among four options.
    \item[H2] Perceived urgency and arousal rise monotonically with the heart rate state.
    \item[H3] Perceived stability falls as ring coherence declines across the variability states.
    \item[H4] The intention to offer support rises as ring coherence declines.
    \item[H5] The display registers low workload on the Task Load Index while returning high clarity and coordination usefulness.
\end{description}

\subsection{Design Rationale}
Three commitments from Science and Technology Studies fixed the design decisions. Because measurement helps constitute the phenomenon it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, we classified heart rate and variability relative to each player's own baseline rather than against an absolute clinical scale. Because the categories an interface installs carry consequences and harden into infrastructure \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, we used a small set of relative visual states and no named emotion. Because any reading of a body is situated and partial \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, and meaning forms in place rather than inside a fixed symbol \cite{suchman_plans_1987, dourish_where_2004}, we left the cues open for interpretation against the surrounding play. This treatment of ambiguity as a resource follows prior evidence that one bodily signal supports many readings depending on its context \cite{howell_biosignals_2016}. We pursued the design itself as inquiry through research creation \cite{amos_kyriaki_2015} and speculative design \cite{wakkary_things_2021}.

\subsection{Icon System and Stimuli}
The stimulus set was the icon system itself. Heart rate, an index of momentary cardiovascular activation, controlled pulse speed, glow, and halo scale. Heart rate variability, a marker of autonomic regulation estimated from the interval between beats \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, controlled the coherence of a surrounding ring, from a smooth continuous halo to a broken and jittering one. Each channel held four states defined relative to baseline, producing 16 combinations through the crossing of four heart rate states and four variability states. We rendered each state as a looping animation of five to seven seconds around a single avatar. Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, a participant who had learned each channel could read a combination not seen before. No state carried an emotion word.

We represented simulated heart-rate activity using four states defined relative to a participant's baseline. The states controlled the icon's pulse speed, glow intensity, and halo expansion. These categories were used as prototype design states rather than medical diagnoses or fixed emotional labels.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to baseline and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to provide medical or emotional diagnoses.}
\label{tab:hr-stimulus-classification}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.19\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{HR state} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{H1: Resting / Low Activation} &
Calm or low cardiovascular activation &
Baseline to $+5$ bpm, or $\leq 10\%$ above baseline &
Slow halo pulse and soft glow \\
\textbf{H2: Focused Activation} &
Engaged, alert, and mildly activated &
$+6$ to $+15$ bpm, or $11$--$25\%$ above baseline &
Medium pulse and clearer glow \\
\textbf{H3: High Activation} &
Intense engagement, pressure, or effort &
$+16$ to $+30$ bpm, or $26$--$45\%$ above baseline &
Fast pulse and stronger glow \\
\textbf{H4: Overdrive Activation} &
Very high activation and possible overload &
More than $+30$ bpm, or more than $45\%$ above baseline &
Rapid pulse, sharp glow, and stronger expansion \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

We represented simulated heart-rate variability using four baseline-relative states. The thresholds were expressed as percentages of baseline HRV using an RMSSD-style design logic. These prototype categories represented changes in coherence, stability, and fragmentation rather than fixed emotional or clinical states.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Heart-rate-variability stimulus classification used in the prototype. Thresholds were defined relative to baseline HRV and were used to structure the simulated visual states rather than to assign clinical or emotional labels.}
\label{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabularx}{\textwidth}{@{}p{0.20\textwidth}YYY@{}}
\toprule
\textbf{HRV state} &
\textbf{Physiological meaning} &
\textbf{Prototype threshold} &
\textbf{Icon effect} \\
\midrule
\textbf{V1: High Coherence} &
High variability and flexible regulation &
$\geq 110\%$ of baseline HRV &
Smooth continuous ring, wide breathing halo, and stable motion \\
\textbf{V2: Stable Variability} &
Normal or baseline variability &
$90$--$109\%$ of baseline HRV &
Clean dotted ring and regular halo motion \\
\textbf{V3: Reduced Variability} &
Lower flexibility and possible physiological strain &
$60$--$89\%$ of baseline HRV &
Denser segmented ring with a slight wobble \\
\textbf{V4: Suppressed / Fragmented Variability} &
Very low variability and reduced regulatory stability &
Less than $60\%$ of baseline HRV &
Broken ring, visible jitter, and irregular opacity \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table*}

Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} summarize the baseline-relative HR and HRV classifications used to generate the sixteen animated stimulus conditions.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Icon.jpg}
  \caption{The icon design evolves through theoretical perspectives from Science and Technology Studies (STS), transforming an abstract sensing field into a human avatar surrounded by a dynamic halo. The final icon visualizes physiological signals as an ambiguous social cue, where halo brightness, pulse, density, and stability vary with heart rate (HR) and heart rate variability (HRV).}
  \Description{A visual design figure showing variations in avatar shapes and layered halo structures used to represent changes in heart rate and heart-rate variability.}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Study Design}
We used a within participants design in which every participant viewed all 16 states, removing between person variation from the central comparisons. This format, in which each viewer judges every icon, matches recent within participants recognition tests of icon sets in HCI \cite{schafer_shape_2023}, and evaluating a heart rate driven display through a dedicated player facing study follows recent work on biofeedback games \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. We administered it online in two phases. The open phase presented 4 states per participant with all labels hidden, and the structured phase presented all 16 states in randomised order. Each participant therefore completed 20 rating trials, yielding 144 trials in the open phase and 576 in the structured phase across the sample, followed by one closing questionnaire per participant.

\subsection{Participants}
We recruited thirty six adults, all of whom completed the study. Twenty one were aged 18 to 24, twelve were aged 25 to 34, two were aged 35 to 44, and one was aged 45 to 54. Gaming background covered four reported levels: 15 regular players, 10 frequent players, 9 casual players, and 2 expert or competitive players. Familiarity with heart rate and variability was slight for 14 participants, moderate for 18, and high for 4. For prior use of wearable devices, 14 participants reported rare use, 7 sometimes, 8 often, and 2 very often, and 5 did not report it. For visual accessibility, 31 reported no known issue, 3 reported motion sensitivity, and 2 reported a colour vision difficulty. Participants took part on a desktop or laptop (27), a phone (6), or a tablet (3). We did not collect gender. Table~\ref{tab:participant-demographics} summarises age and device use.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{Participant demographics and device use ($N=36$).}
\label{tab:participant-demographics}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{5pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.08}
\begin{tabularx}{\columnwidth}{@{}Xr@{}}
\toprule
Category & $n$ (\%) \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{2}{@{}l}{\textit{Age group}} \\
18--24 & 21 (58.3\%) \\
25--34 & 12 (33.3\%) \\
35--44 & 2 (5.6\%) \\
45--54 & 1 (2.8\%) \\
\addlinespace
\multicolumn{2}{@{}l}{\textit{Device used}} \\
Desktop/laptop & 27 (75.0\%) \\
Phone & 6 (16.7\%) \\
Tablet & 3 (8.3\%) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\subsection{Apparatus and Materials}
We ran the study in a standard web browser and generated each state with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, presenting one avatar inside a simulated team status panel. The heart rate and variability states were synthetic and produced the same animation logic the deployed interface would use, which isolated the reading of the display from the demands of live play and gave every participant an identical stimulus set. We did not require live capture at this stage, since streaming a wearable's heart rate into a game engine is already established \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}.

\subsection{Procedure}
Each session opened with consent and a short demographic form, then moved through the two phases. In the open phase, and with every label hidden, participants viewed a state, wrote in free text what it conveyed, rated their confidence, selected the closest option from a set of plain descriptions, and rated perceived activation and perceived stability on seven point scales. This followed the open logic of graphical symbol comprehension, where an unprompted answer reveals what a symbol communicates without text \cite{iso_9186_2014}. In the structured phase, participants saw all 16 states in randomised order and, for each, identified the heart rate state from four options and the variability state from four options, rated clarity, urgency, perceived stability, usefulness for coordination, and intention to offer support on seven point scales, and completed a seven point pictorial affective rating. We closed each session with a questionnaire that recorded workload, confidence, visual comfort, and five open responses covering the most helpful feature, the most confusing states, whether the display felt useful or invasive, willingness to use it in cooperative play, and suggested improvements.

\subsection{Measures}
We measured legibility as identification accuracy for the heart rate state and the variability state in the structured phase, each scored against the 25 percent expected from selecting among four options, following the forced choice recognition format used in recent icon studies \cite{schafer_shape_2023}. We assessed affective interpretation with the Self-Assessment Manikin, a nonverbal pictorial scale for valence, arousal, and dominance that avoids emotion vocabulary \cite{bradley_measuring_1994}, administered on seven points. We assessed workload with three items of the NASA Task Load Index, namely mental demand, effort, and frustration \cite{hart_development_1988}, on seven point scales. Five further seven point items captured the social reading of each state, including clarity, urgency, perceived stability, usefulness for coordination, and intention to offer support, and the open phase added a free text interpretation and a forced match to a plain description.

\subsection{Analysis}
We compared identification accuracy for each channel against the 25 percent chance level. We tested the intended perceptual mappings with planned within participants linear trend analyses. For each outcome, every participant contributed a single linear trend slope across the ordered states, and we tested the slopes against zero with two sided one sample t tests, Holm corrected across the five planned tests. We summarised workload and the seven point rating items with descriptive statistics. We analysed the free text interpretations and the five open responses with reflexive thematic analysis \cite{braun_using_2006}, applied in line with current guidance on thematic analysis practice in HCI \cite{bowman_thematic_2023}, which produced the account of how participants read the states and the boundaries they placed on physiological visibility.
\section{Results}
Thirty six participants produced 576 recognition trials in the structured phase and 144 free interpretations in the open phase. Each participant contributed one linear trend slope per outcome, and we tested each slope against zero with a two sided one sample t test, with Holm correction across the five planned tests. Figure~\ref{fig:procedure} summarises the procedure. We report legibility, then the intended perceptual mappings, then performance across the full grid, then usability and workload, then the qualitative findings.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_2_Experimental_Procedure_600dpi.png}
  \caption{The two-phase online procedure: an open phase presenting 4 unlabeled states per participant, followed by a structured phase presenting all 16 states in randomised order.}
  \Description{A diagram of the study procedure, showing consent and demographics, the open phase with four unlabeled states, the structured phase with all sixteen states, and a closing questionnaire.}
  \label{fig:procedure}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Icon Legibility (H1)}
Before any label was shown, participants' best-match interpretations of the four unaided anchor states in the open phase averaged 81.9 percent correct against the 20 percent chance level for the five-option match, indicating that the design read correctly even without prior exposure to the full state set. In the structured phase, participants identified the heart rate state on 84.2 percent of trials and the variability state on 80.6 percent, both far above the 25 percent expected from a choice among four options, supporting H1. Accuracy was even rather than carried by easy extremes. Participants recognised heart rate states at 84.7, 90.3, 82.6, and 79.2 percent from H1 to H4, and variability states at 79.2, 82.6, 79.2, and 81.2 percent from V1 to V4. Errors concentrated on neighbouring levels: H4 was read as H3 on 18.1 percent of its trials and V1 as V2 on 20.1 percent, while confusion between non adjacent states stayed at or below 1.4 percent. No state fell below 79 percent. Identifying both channels correctly on the same trial, a harder sixteen-way judgement, still reached 68 percent against a 6.25 percent chance level. Figure~\ref{fig:legibility} reports recognition per state against chance.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_3_Icon_Legibility_and_Recognition_600dpi.png}
  \caption{Identification accuracy for each heart rate state and each variability state in the structured phase, against the 25 percent chance level for a four-option choice.}
  \Description{A bar chart of recognition accuracy for the four heart rate states and four variability states, each well above the 25 percent chance line.}
  \label{fig:legibility}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Perceived Activation and Urgency (H2)}
The heart rate channel carried activation as intended. Each step up the heart rate state raised perceived urgency by 1.27 points on the seven point scale (95 percent CI 1.21 to 1.32), $t(35)=47.49$, $p<.001$, with condition means of 1.94, 3.15, 4.49, and 5.72 from H1 to H4. Rated arousal rose in step, by 1.35 points per level (95 percent CI 1.31 to 1.39), $t(35)=70.41$, $p<.001$, from 1.76 to 5.77. Both trends survived correction and support H2. Figure~\ref{fig:mappings}A shows the two curves with within participant confidence intervals.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_4_Validation_of_Perceptual_Mappings_600dpi.png}
  \caption{Validation of the intended perceptual mappings. (A) Perceived urgency and SAM arousal rise monotonically with heart rate state. (B) Perceived stability falls and support intention rises as ring coherence declines across the variability states.}
  \Description{Two line charts with within-participant confidence intervals: urgency and arousal rising across the four heart rate states, and stability falling while support intention rises across the four variability states.}
  \label{fig:mappings}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Perceived Stability and the Call for Support (H3 and H4)}
The variability channel carried regulation as intended. Each step toward a more fragmented ring lowered perceived stability by 1.33 points (95 percent CI 1.29 to 1.37), $t(35)=70.50$, $p<.001$, with means of 6.78, 5.56, 4.12, and 2.82 from V1 to V4, supporting H3. Over the same states the intention to offer support rose by 0.72 points per level (95 percent CI 0.66 to 0.78), $t(35)=24.42$, $p<.001$, from 3.12 to 5.25, supporting H4. Rated control moved on the same axis, falling by 0.71 points per level (95 percent CI 0.66 to 0.76), $t(35)=29.74$, $p<.001$. Table~\ref{tab:trend-tests} lists all five trend tests, and Figure~\ref{fig:mappings}B plots the variability panel.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\caption{Planned within-subject linear-trend tests of the intended perceptual mappings. Effect is the mean rating change per one-level step, with direction noted. Holm-adjusted $p$ corrects for the five planned tests ($df=35$).}
\label{tab:trend-tests}
\small
\begin{tabular}{@{}llcccc@{}}
\toprule
Outcome & Predictor & Effect per level & 95\% CI & $t$ & Adjusted $p$ \\
\midrule
Perceived urgency (rises) & HR state & 1.27 & 1.21 to 1.32 & 47.49 & $<.001$ \\
SAM arousal (rises) & HR state & 1.35 & 1.31 to 1.39 & 70.41 & $<.001$ \\
Perceived stability (falls) & HRV fragmentation & 1.33 & 1.29 to 1.37 & 70.50 & $<.001$ \\
SAM dominance (falls) & HRV fragmentation & 0.71 & 0.66 to 0.76 & 29.74 & $<.001$ \\
Support intention (rises) & HRV fragmentation & 0.72 & 0.66 to 0.78 & 24.42 & $<.001$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}

\subsection{Performance Across the Full Grid}
Because we composed the two channels rather than assigning unrelated appearances, legibility could have collapsed where an active pulse met a fragmented ring. It did not. Recognition held across all 16 combinations, with no cell falling to chance and the hardest cells confined to adjacent middle states, which confirms that participants read the two channels independently rather than memorising whole icons. Figure~\ref{fig:conditions} shows performance for every combination.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure_5_Condition_Level_Performance_600dpi.png}
  \caption{Identification accuracy for all 16 heart rate by variability combinations. No combination fell to chance, and the hardest cells were confined to adjacent middle states.}
  \Description{A heatmap of recognition accuracy across the four heart rate states by four variability states, with all cells well above chance and the lowest values concentrated among adjacent middle states.}
  \label{fig:conditions}
\end{figure*}

\subsection{Usability and Workload (H5)}
The display read clearly and cost little effort. Clarity averaged 5.97 of 7 and coordination usefulness 5.79, while the three Task Load Index items stayed low, with mental demand at 3.19, effort at 3.28, and frustration at 2.06. Overall confidence was 5.83 and visual comfort 5.86. These results support H5. Table~\ref{tab:usability-workload} reports each measure with its 95 percent confidence interval.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\caption{Overall usability and workload ratings.}
\label{tab:usability-workload}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.08}
\begin{tabularx}{\columnwidth}{@{}Xcc@{}}
\toprule
Measure & Mean & 95\% CI \\
\midrule
Clarity & 5.97/7 & 5.92--6.01 \\
Coordination usefulness & 5.79/7 & 5.74--5.83 \\
Mental demand & 3.19/7 & 2.98--3.41 \\
Effort & 3.28/7 & 3.04--3.52 \\
Frustration & 2.06/7 & 1.83--2.28 \\
Overall confidence & 5.83/7 & 5.61--6.05 \\
Visual comfort & 5.86/7 & 5.58--6.14 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabularx}
\end{table}

\subsection{How Participants Read the States}
The open responses explain the recognition scores. Participants reported reading the two channels separately, using pulse speed and glow for activation and the ring for stability, which is what the compositional design intended. The pulsing centre and the intact or broken ring were named repeatedly as the features that carried the most meaning. The states participants found hardest were adjacent middle combinations, and one recurring difficulty was telling an intentionally dotted ring from a lightly broken one, which matches the neighbour confusions in the recognition data.

\subsection{Usefulness, Boundaries, and Willingness to Use}
Participants judged the display useful for cooperative play, and several tied that judgement to its refusal to name a feeling, valuing that it showed a pattern rather than a verdict. The same responses set conditions. Participants asked for control over who can see their status, for the display to stay optional and mutable, and for the animation to remain subtle during normal play, and some described unrestricted visibility as potentially invasive. Willingness to adopt was expressed most often for cooperative modes where a teammate cannot pause to explain their state, and was framed as an added cue rather than proof of how someone feels.

\section{System Design}
\subsection{System Overview}
We built a multiplayer interface that displays physiological signals from wearable devices during cooperative play. The system converts physiological measurements into visual cues embedded in each player's avatar icon, so a teammate can notice a change in another player's physiological state without leaving the game.

The architecture has three layers: physiological data acquisition from wearable sensors, a processing layer that converts physiological measures into interface states, and a visual interface layer that renders those states on player avatar icons. We chose subtle visual modification over numerical displays or text labels, following evidence that ambient information visualization can convey complex data while preserving the interpretive flexibility of what it displays \cite{dourish_where_2004}.

\subsection{Physiological Data Acquisition}
We drew physiological data from wearable devices, in particular the Apple Watch. Its photoplethysmography sensors track cardiovascular activity continuously by detecting changes in blood volume beneath the skin, and applications can access heart rate and heart rate variability through the HealthKit framework.

Heart rate expresses transient cardiovascular stimulation as beats per minute. Heart rate variability, commonly used as a marker of autonomic nervous system function, expresses the fluctuation between successive heartbeats and can reveal cognitive load, stress, and emotional arousal during interactive tasks \cite{mandryk_fuzzy_2007}. The system retrieves HR and HRV measurements from each player's wearable device at regular intervals and transmits them to the game environment as the basis for the interface visualization.

\subsection{Physiological Signal Interpretation}
The interface never shows a raw physiological measurement. Instead, the interpretation layer converts physiological data into a small set of interface states, concentrating on relative change rather than trying to infer a specific emotional state. Mapping physiological data directly onto emotional categories has been criticised in both physiological computing and Science and Technology Studies \cite{haraway_situated_1988, bowker_sorting_2008}, so the system classifies patterns of cardiovascular activation from heart rate and heart rate variability relative to each player's own baseline rather than an absolute scale. Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} give the resulting four-state classification for each channel and the visual effect each state produces.

Establishing that baseline is itself a design decision the system has to make explicit rather than assume. We treat it in two stages rather than as a single fixed number. A short calibration window at the start of a session, during a pre-match lobby or loading screen when players are typically at rest, sets an initial resting HR and HRV; a rolling personal baseline then updates across sessions to track ordinary drift from fatigue, hydration, or time of day, so classification stays relative to how a player usually presents rather than to one measurement taken once. Before enough personal history accumulates, the calibration window falls back to broad population norms, so the system converges to a personal baseline over a player's first few sessions rather than requiring it upfront.

\subsection{Avatar Icon Visualization}
A visual halo surrounds each player's avatar icon and changes in response to physiological cues. Brightness, pulse rhythm, and structural stability vary with the underlying HR and HRV states: a steady, gently lit halo under stable physiology, a faster and brighter pulse as activation rises, and a more fragmented, jittering ring as variability drops. The halo carries no numerical indicator or text label, so a player reads it in relation to the ongoing match and their teammates' behaviour rather than as a fixed diagnosis.

\subsection{From Validated Stimuli to a Live Engine Implementation}
We built the stimulus set for the study in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript because a browser gave every participant an identical, network-independent animation. A deployed version needs the same visual logic running inside a game engine and driven by a live wearable feed rather than a scripted state, and we designed the classification in Tables~\ref{tab:hr-stimulus-classification} and~\ref{tab:hrv-stimulus-classification} so that translation is direct rather than a reimplementation from scratch.

In Unity, pulse duration, glow intensity, and halo scale correspond to float parameters on an Animator Controller (for example \texttt{hrState} and \texttt{pulseSpeed}), which drive a Shader Graph material on the halo mesh; ring form, stability, and jitter correspond to the emission rate, noise module, and opacity curve of a Particle System keyed to the four HRV states. Each avatar's halo becomes a self contained prefab that reads two small state values, heart rate state and variability state, and interpolates between the tabulated visual parameters rather than switching between sixteen hand authored animations, which keeps the sixteen combinations compositional in the engine the same way they were compositional in the study stimuli. The same parameterization holds in Unreal Engine, where a Material Parameter Collection exposes the equivalent floats to a Niagara particle system and an Animation Blueprint, so the mapping is not tied to one proprietary pipeline.

Feeding real HR and HRV into these parameters requires a streaming bridge between the wearable and the engine, which prior work has already shown is practical: Unity applications can ingest consumer smartwatch heart rate over a live connection \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}. For heart rate variability, the engine would compute an RMSSD style measure from the incoming beat to beat intervals and apply the same baseline relative classification used in the Methodology, so the state a live player sees is produced by the identical logic that produced the validated stimuli rather than a separate implementation that only approximates it.

Because each avatar's halo is local state driven by two small integers, propagating it across a multiplayer session only requires replicating those two values per player, for instance through Unity's Netcode for GameObjects or an equivalent networked variable in another engine, rather than streaming raw physiological time series between clients. A teammate's client renders the halo locally from a synced state index, so no continuous biometric stream needs to leave the sending player's device, which keeps the bandwidth footprint small and limits what a deployment actually has to protect to two numbers per player rather than a raw physiological feed.

\subsection{Interface Integration in Multiplayer Gameplay}
Teammates' avatar icons, each with its physiological halo, appear alongside the rest of the multiplayer interface throughout a match. The halo updates continuously as physiological measurements change, and stays deliberately unobtrusive so that it functions as a peripheral cue rather than competing for attention with core gameplay. The design targets moments where players already have to interpret a teammate's behaviour under uncertainty, for instance judging whether a partner is struggling, hesitating, or increasingly engaged, and the physiological icon adds one further layer to that ongoing interpretation without asserting a definitive emotional state.
\section{Discussion}

The study set out to learn whether a teammate's body could be made readable at a distance without naming a feeling. The recognition scores and the five trend tests answer that question in the affirmative and let us say more precisely what such a display does and does not do.

\subsection{Legibility Without Emotional Labels}
The central result is that an interface can withhold every emotion word and still be read. Participants recovered the heart rate state and the variability state from animation alone at 84.2 and 80.6 percent, and their readings ordered themselves with the underlying states across all five planned tests. This matters because it separates two things the shared physiology literature has tended to bundle. Systems that name an emotion assume the naming is warranted \cite{picard_affective_1997, mandryk_fuzzy_2007}, and systems that report warmer feeling between partners rarely check that the viewer can identify the state on show \cite{slovak_understanding_2012, hirsch_my_2023, hassan_augmenting_2024}. Our contribution is to close that gap directly: a label free display of cardiovascular state is legible to a reader, and legible in a graded rather than a categorical way. The design withholds the verdict yet still communicates, which is the outcome the affective ambiguity argument predicts but had not tested at the level of identification accuracy \cite{howell_biosignals_2016, howell_tensions_2018}.

\subsection{Two Separable Physiological Channels}
Almost all prior work in this space transmits heart rate on its own. We encoded heart rate and heart rate variability as two visual channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence, and the two were read as distinct social properties. Activation drove urgency and arousal, with each step raising perceived urgency by 1.27 points, while fragmentation drove a different axis, lowering perceived stability by 1.33 points per step and raising the intention to help. The two indices are physiologically distinct, since rate reflects momentary activation and variability reflects the autonomic regulation beneath it \cite{thayer_model_2000, porges_polyvagal_2007, shaffer_overview_2017}, and the study shows this distinction survives the translation into a single icon. Performance held across all sixteen combinations rather than collapsing where an urgent pulse met a broken ring, which indicates that participants read the channels compositionally rather than memorising whole icons. For designers of physiological interfaces, this is evidence that more than one bodily signal can be carried at once, provided each is mapped to a visual variable a viewer can isolate.

\subsection{From Reading a State to Intending to Act}
A display meant for coordination has to do more than convey a state; it has to move a teammate toward a response. The variability channel did exactly that. As ring coherence broke down, the intention to offer support rose monotonically, from 3.12 to 5.25. This links a physiological cue to a prosocial disposition rather than only to felt closeness, and it speaks to the coordination problem that motivates the work, where teammates who cannot read one another misjudge who needs help \cite{tan_role_2014, bisberg_communication_2025, leavitt_ping_2016}. The result suggests that a shared bodily signal can act as a lightweight call for coordination, not merely as a channel for empathy, which extends the connection oriented framing of earlier heart rate sharing systems \cite{hassib_heartchat_2017, janssen_intimate_2010, jarvela_augmented_2021}.

\subsection{Ambiguity as a Designed Resource}
The design treated openness as a feature and grounded that decision in Science and Technology Studies. Because measurement helps constitute what it appears to record \cite{nelson_meeting_1996}, because the categories an interface fixes carry consequences \cite{bowker_sorting_2008, winner_artifacts_1980}, and because any reading of a body is situated \cite{haraway_situated_1988}, the interface offered graded cues to be interpreted in place rather than settled verdicts. The qualitative responses show this reasoning reached the participants: several valued that the display showed a pattern rather than a diagnosis, and read each state against the imagined match rather than as a fixed label. The theoretical contribution is therefore twofold. We offer a method that turns abstract STS commitments into concrete visual choices, and we provide evidence that the resulting artifact communicates, which moves the ambiguity argument from a critical stance into a validated design position \cite{howell_biosignals_2016, wakkary_things_2021}.

\subsection{Design Implications}
Four implications follow for physiological interfaces in shared settings. First, encoding relative to a personal baseline, rather than against an absolute scale, kept states legible without asserting a clinical reading, and is a defensible default for social displays. Second, separable visual variables allow a single icon to carry two bodily signals, which suggests headroom for richer physiological communication than the single heart rate channel now common. Third, withholding emotion labels cost nothing in legibility and gained user acceptance, which indicates that graded, open cues are a viable alternative to categorical affect display. Fourth, and most consequential, control over visibility is a precondition rather than a refinement, since participants accepted the display only when it remained optional, mutable, and subtle.

\subsection{Making the Body Visible Redistributes Accountability}
The boundaries participants drew are themselves a finding. Making a bodily signal visible changes the informational terms of play, and with them how blame, hesitation, and effort are read among teammates, which is precisely the redistribution of accountability that a politics of artifacts anticipates \cite{winner_artifacts_1980}. Participants asked for consent over who can see their status and for the ability to withdraw it, which recasts privacy from an afterthought into part of the interface's core logic. A deployed version would need to treat the right to be illegible as seriously as the capacity to be read.

\section{Limitations}

Several limitations bound these claims. The study validated interpretation of the visual language, not the fidelity of live capture or behaviour during play. Stimuli presented simulated cardiovascular states rather than signals streamed from a worn device, so the results establish that the icons are readable, not that a real Apple Watch feed would produce equally clean states under motion and noise; the streaming pipeline needed for that test is established but was not exercised here \cite{liang_harnessing_2024}. Reading icons in a status panel also differs from reading them under the attentional demand of a live match, and although workload was low in isolation, it may rise when the display competes with gameplay \cite{tu_biofeedback_2025}. The design is also tied to one vendor's sensing stack: it assumes every player wears an Apple Watch, which is not true of a general multiplayer audience spread across PC, console, and mixed device ecosystems. A deployed version needs a fallback for players without a supported wearable, most simply a neutral, unanimated avatar, and needs to generalise the baseline relative classification to other consumer sensors, whose photoplethysmography accuracy and HRV estimation vary by device.

The design was evaluated in a single online session, so retention, habituation, and whether the cue helps or distracts over extended play remain open. The study measured legibility and the intention to act, not coordination outcomes; it does not show that teams using the display make faster or better decisions, which requires a behavioural study during real cooperative play. The sample of 36 participants skewed toward ages 18 to 34, was self selected, and completed the task mostly on desktop, so generalisation to broader populations and to phone or in headset contexts is untested. All ratings were self reported, without a physiological ground truth for the viewer, and the interpretation of heart rate variability is context dependent, so the relative states necessarily simplify a signal that is noisier in the field. Finally, the privacy concerns participants raised were surfaced qualitatively rather than designed for and evaluated, and a real deployment would need to resolve consent, data ownership, and control over visibility before these findings could be carried into practice.
\section{Conclusion}
Distributed play removes the body from social interaction. Teammates who would read one another's effort and strain at a glance in a shared room are left, across a network, inferring those states from text, voice, and avatar motion, and the resulting misreadings seed misplaced blame and broken coordination. This paper asked whether the missing bodily layer could be returned as a readable cue without collapsing it into a named emotion, and it answered by designing a peripheral avatar display that renders wearable heart rate and heart rate variability as two visual channels, pulse and brightness against ring coherence, on principles drawn from Science and Technology Studies.

An online study with 36 participants showed that the display communicates. Participants recovered the heart rate state and the variability state from animation alone at 84.2 and 80.6 percent, far above chance, and their readings ordered themselves with the underlying states across five planned trend tests: activation was read as urgency and arousal, declining ring coherence as lower stability, and fragmentation as a stronger call to help. The display cost little effort and was judged useful for cooperative play, and it did all of this while withholding every emotion word, which turns the argument that biosignals are ambiguous social cues from a critical stance into a validated design position.

The contribution is threefold: a wearable driven interaction technique that makes a distant teammate legible in a shared interactive space, a method that translates Science and Technology Studies commitments about measurement, classification, and situated knowledge into concrete visual choices, and evidence that a label free physiological display is legible, graded, and light enough to use. The boundaries participants placed on physiological visibility are part of the result, and they mark the immediate path forward. What remains is to move from a validated icon to a played system: to drive the display from live wearable data, to test it during real cooperative play against coordination outcomes rather than interpretation alone, and to build consent and control over visibility into its core so that the right to be illegible is held as firmly as the capacity to be read.
\section{Generative AI Use Disclosure}
ChatGPT was used for language editing and restructuring text drafted by the author. It was not used to generate the research idea, conceptual framework, study design, data, results, or citations. The author reviewed and takes responsibility for all content.
